RESEARCH POLICY
SCHOOL OF MANAGEMENT
B M L MUNJAL UNIVERSITY




POLICY DOCUMENT

2015






















Introduction
This research policy document is available to all the full time faculty members of School of
Management and explains the research opportunities and funding facilities available at School of
Management.


BMU’s Mission Statement

BML Munjal University aims to be a leading University for the quality and impact of its
teaching, research and linkages with major stakeholders. The focus of the University is to find
creative solutions to problems through application of knowledge. The University aims to create a
talented community of students and faculty who excel in teaching, learning and research, in a
creative and stimulating environment. The University seeks partnership with other institutions
for development of science, technology and arts in the global co ntext.

BMU actively promotes academic research in order to be consistent with its mission of pursuing
excellence in management research and the commitment to continually benchmark best -in-class
institutions. Specifically, it is recognized that BMU can gai n the following benefits if its faculty
pursue rigorous academic research:

1. Enrich its teaching standards . Research helps a faculty member continuously increase
one’s breadth and depth of knowledge of the subject thereby improving one’s ability to
provide better inputs, clarify doubts and introduce contemporary insights in class.
2. Attract good faculty and retain them . Accomplished academics seek to work in those
schools that give them excellent support for academic research in the form o f adequate
funding, time and infrastructure as well as research environment . Promoting academic
research can help BMU attract reputed academics and thought leaders either as full -time
or as visiting faculty . Conversely, not promoting research can provoke e xistin g faculty to
leave the university in search of better opportunities.
3. Inspire students to conduct practitioner oriented or scholarly research in association
with faculty members and enhance their understanding of the subject.

3. Inspire students to conduct practitioner oriented or scholarly research in association
with faculty members and enhance their understanding of the subject.
4. Improve BMU’s national a nd international ranking . The quantity and quality of
research done by a university’s faculty is a key component in university/business school
ranking s.
5. BMU ’s recognition as an academic institution of excellence will increase if its
research output is increased in terms of both quantity and quality, and so will its
reputation amongst peer institutions. This is consistent with BMU ’s aim of benchmarking
itself with best -in-class institutions.
6. Receive and maintain the AACSB Accreditation successfully . Rece iving this prestigious
accreditation means that BMU can more easily partner with best -in-class institutions across
the world . The AACSB accreditation process itself requires that BMU maintain a certain

accreditation means that BMU can more easily partner with best -in-class institutions across
the world . The AACSB accreditation process itself requires that BMU maintain a certain
level of research output. It also requires that at lea st a certain percentage of the members of
the faculty body be “Academically Qualified” by publishing at a certain minimum rate in
peer-reviewed academic journals. For this reason, BMU requires to promote academic
research amongst its faculty.

Research Promotion Committee (RPC)

The Research Promotion Committee (RPC) is a sub -committee of the faculty council of BMU ,
constituted to facilitate research at BMU in accordance with the above mission . In other words, the
RPC serves as BMU ’s vehicle to promote research. It consists of faculty members appointed by the
Dean of School of Management at BMU .

The mission of the RPC is aligned with the mission of BMU and is expressed in the following
mission statement:

Mission Statement of the RPC, SOM -BMU

To promote and nurture academic as well as applied research with the objective of addressing
issues facing the academic community, industry and government and general community at
large .

Members of the Research Promotion Committee (RPC)

The current RPC wil l comprise of the following faculty members :

a) Dean - SOM
b) Prof. S R Viswanath
c) Prof. Jaskiran Arora
d) Prof. Sanghamitra Bhattachar yya

The RPC Policy Document

The current RPC wil l comprise of the following faculty members :

a) Dean - SOM
b) Prof. S R Viswanath
c) Prof. Jaskiran Arora
d) Prof. Sanghamitra Bhattachar yya

The RPC Policy Document

The policy document of the RPC describes the RPC’s mission, policies and guidelines. This
document is aligned with the stated mission of BMU. It is prepared and updated following a five
step proce dure:

Step 1: The RPC meets and reviews existing practices, policies, guidelines and norms related to
research at BMU . Based on the discussions, the RPC identifies the changes to be made and
prepares a draft policy document. The draft document is then reviewed by all the committee
members and any corrections deemed necessary are carried out by mutual consent.

Step 2: The RPC circulates the draft policy document among members of the BMU faculty
council for their review, feedback and recommendations.

Step 2: The RPC circulates the draft policy document among members of the BMU faculty
council for their review, feedback and recommendations.

Step 3: The inputs and changes suggest ed by the faculty council are then assessed by the RPC
keeping the BMU mission in perspective and a revised document is prepared and submitted to
the D ean of School of Management for a second review and presentation to the Board.

Step 4: The members of the Board are invited to critically assess the document and give their
feedb ack in the form of suggestions for modifications. The inputs received from the Board are
discussed by the RPC internally in the presence of the Dean and corrections to the document
are made as appropriate.


Step 5: The document is finally re -presented to th e Board for formal approval, after which it is
formalized as the Policy Document of the RPC.


Activities of the RPC

In accordance with its mission, RPC’s activities are organized around the following themes:

Activities of the RPC

In accordance with its mission, RPC’s activities are organized around the following themes:

1. Organize BMU research seminars , providing a platform for faculty and students to
come together to discuss current research.
2. Maintain a series of Working Papers written by faculty and publish the results at
appropriate platforms
3. Facilitate the publication of papers in peer -reviewed journals
4. Facilitate the participation of BMU faculty in academic conferences and seminars
5. Organize workshops related to research methodology and contemporary research in
all disciplines in management
6. Organize academic research conferences at BMU
7. Facilitate funding of internal research projects .
8. Facilitate applications to externally funded research projects.
9. Publish the BMU Research Newsletter .
10. Support Research Centers within the School of Management to achieve their goals
11. Maintain and continuously update the Researc h Policy Document .

Each of these activities is elaborated upon in the following sub -sections.

Research Seminars at BMU

The RPC organizes research seminars by faculty members and scholars visiting from other
institutions. The research seminar is meant to provide a platform to present a research project
within the community. Invitations to participate are sent to all faculty and student s of BMU and
institutions in NCR such as the Management Development Institute, Gurgaon . The RPC also
invites we ll-known academics from other schools in India and abroad t o present their research at
BMU and infuse different perspectives on contemporary topics of research interest.

These seminars are held on Friday afternoons at 3 PM and are usually 45 minutes long.

These seminars are held on Friday afternoons at 3 PM and are usually 45 minutes long.

Seminar presentations can be on completed and submitted research papers or on work -in-
progress that can benefit from the inputs and insights of colleagues. Seminars are ‘knowledge
exchange’ programmes to help the presenter gain fresh ideas and direction to his work, and to
educate the fraternity on research topics they may not be familiar with.

There is no deadline or a time window for registration; a faculty member can approach the RPC
to register, whenever he feels his work has reached a stage where he can deliver a seminar and
publish a working paper. A suitable date will be allotted to the faculty member . It must be noted
that seminar dates are fixed in advance in consultation with the graduate and undergraduate


program office to ensure that attendanc e at the seminar is maximized The RPC aims to conduct
at least 20 research seminars annually .

Working Paper Series

It is desirable that each faculty member at BMU publi shes at least one working paper each year.
This will help us nourish a research environment at BMU apart from stimulating the ongoing
research efforts of individual faculty member s. Publishing working papers is aligned with
BMU ’s mission since it helps foster continuous learning and reflection and is a stepping stone to
publishing pap ers in good peer -reviewed journals.

All papers written by faculty members would automatically become a part of the School’s
working paper series

Journal Paper Publication

All papers written by faculty members would automatically become a part of the School’s
working paper series

Journal Paper Publication

It is necessary for BMU faculty members to publish in well -recognized peer -reviewe d academic
journals in order to pursue BMU’s mission of being a leading University for the quality and
impact of its research . The RPC assists faculty in doing this. There is no formal process for
submitting to a journal and authors are free to choose the journals they wish to submit to. To help
discriminate between journals in terms of their quality, BMU refers to three different journal
ranking lists popularly used across the world. These are:

a. The ABDC Journal Quality List published by the Australian Business Deans Council.
b. The ABS Journal Quality Guide published by the Association of Business Schools
(UK).
c. The Ranked Journal List of the ERA (Excellence in Research for Australia) initiative of
the Australian Research Council of the Government of Austra lia. This is also referred to
as the ERA List .
d. Imperial List of Approved Journals
e. List of Journals Approved by IIMs

The university itself maintains a comprehensive list of ranking of journals in all disciplines of
management. This includes Indian and Asian journals that are usually not included in the above
lists.

The author of a journal paper is eligible to receive credits upon receipt of formal acceptance from
the journal, at the end of the journal’s review process1. The above mentioned three lists and
university’s ranking of journals are used as references for awarding faculty credits. Since the

1 The credits are awarded to a paper in the assessment year in which the formal acceptance is received from the
journal. The journal’s issue may actually get published in the subsequent assessment year, or even two assessment
years later. However, the accounting for calculating credits and BMU’s research output will be done only in the year
in which the acceptance is received from the journal and not the subsequent year in which the paper actually gets
published .


rank earned by a journal may differ across lists, the best rank attributed to a journal across all the
three lists is taken as its rank for determining the credits. For example, if two of the lists rank a
journal as B, but one of them ranks it as A, then the journal will be considered an A ranked
journal for faculty credit computation purposes. The relationship between a journal’s rank and
the faculty credits awarded for a paper published in it, is explained in Appendix A.

Soft copies of all four lists will be circulated to the faculty council from time to time, and are
also available with the RPC at any ti me. The RPC recommends that BMU authors refer to thes e
lists when they prepare and submit to journals. Publications in web based platforms are not
awarded any faculty credit, as they are often not refereed or screened properly. However, the
university makes exceptions when necessary.

Best Paper Awards

In order to encourage good research we propose to SOM -BMU three awards in the following
categories:

1. Best Working Paper Award
2. Best Published Paper Award
3. Best Case Award

All submissions to the RPC would be considered for these awards. There is no need for a
separate submission. Winners of these awards would be selected by the Dean after consultations
with the Board of Studies.


Participation of Faculty members in Externa l Academic Conferences, Seminars and
Workshops

Participation of Faculty members in Externa l Academic Conferences, Seminars and
Workshops

BMU encourages its faculty members to present papers in academic research conferences
conducted by other institutions and attend external seminars and workshops to continuously
upgrade their knowledge base. To register for these events as a BMU faculty member, approvals
are needed from the D ean of School of Management , BMU . The expenses such as registration,
travel, lodging and boarding can be claimed by faculty member against his Professional
Development Fun d (PDF, see Appendix D for more details).

For presentations in conferences outside India, faculty members must submit the full paper or
case (that they intend to present) to the SOM -BMU before they leave for the conference. This
must be done even if the c onference acceptance was based on the submission of an abstract. The
submission to the school can be done after the acceptance is received, but must be done before
traveling to the conference. If the expenditure requirement exceeds the balance PDF amount i n
the faculty member’s account, the faculty member can place a request to the Dean to sanction a
larger amount.





Workshops on Research

Workshops on Research

For the benefit of BMU faculty and students, the RPC organizes workshops by seasoned
researchers from time -to-time. These researchers may be from within or outside India. The
workshops aim to produce tangible research output within their time frames. It is highly
desirable that faculty members take advantage of these workshops to boost th eir own research
work and help raise the level of academic research at BMU .

Some examples of workshop themes are:

1. Formulating Research Problems
2. Empirical Finance
3. Advanced Econometric
4. How to do Qualitative Research
5. The Art of Publishing in Reputed Jjournals.

Academic Conferences at BMU

1. Formulating Research Problems

Academic Conferences at BMU

To increase its networking with peer institutions, RPC will organize and/or lend its support to
organizing of academic research conferences within the School of Management from time to
time. The organizing of such a conference requires a dedicated team of faculty members, staff
and students. Hence, it is also possible that sometimes the RPC may not be directly involved
with a conference at BMU .

Internally Funded Grants Research Projects

Faculty members are encouraged to use internal research grants (See Appendix B for more
details) to conduct research projects in areas of their choice.

Exte rnally Funded Research Projects

Faculty members are encouraged to use internal research grants (See Appendix B for more
details) to conduct research projects in areas of their choice.

Exte rnally Funded Research Projects

BMU faculty members are also encouraged to pursue externally funded research projects. In
addition to serving the larger society, undertaking such projects can help a faculty member
develop his depth and breadth of learning in a given area, publish in journals and generally build
his research network. It helps BMU by gaining recognition as a significant contributor to the
knowledge community.

The RPC will work on building a detailed database of information regarding the different
research funding agenc ies across the world from whom such projects can be sought. Some
examples of such bodies in India are the AICTE, UGC, NSE, the ISRO, the CSIR, the DSIR and
the Ministry for Environment and Forests of the Government of India. This database will be

regarding data collection and travel related expenses should be clearly specified in the
application (Annexure 1) for approval of Research Grant to the extent possible.

GUIDELINES & FORMS FOR LARGE GRANT RESEARCH PROJECT (LGRP)
7. Objective

School of Management
BML MUNJAL UNIVERSITY
GUIDELINES & FORMS FOR LARGE GRANT RESEARCH PROJECT (LGRP)
7. Objective

The objective of Large Grant Research Project (LGRP) is to encourage research by faculty at
BMU by way of providing financial support for research in various management domains.
The goal of the LGRP is to support faculty to publish their research in high -quality peer-
reviewed International Journals/ Ca se Clearing Houses.

8. Grant Entitlement / Rules

a) One time grant of Rs.2,00,000.00 (Rupees Two Lakhs) will be awarded for an LGRP.
b) The duration of the proposed study will be a maximum of one year from the day of
proposal approval.
c) Not more than Rs. 1,00,00 0.00 (extendable to Rs.1,50,000.00, in special case involving
substantial field research) of the grant should be used for travelling.
d) A faculty member will be permitted to be away from the Institute for a maximum of 20
days including the dates of travel. When a faculty member is on research travel under
LGRP, in the absence of bills, TA/DA will be paid as per his/her entitlement from the
respective LGRP account.
e) Expenses under ‘travel’ should primarily be used for data collection purpose. Details
regarding data collection and travel related expenses should be clearly specified in the
application (Annexure 1) for approval of Research Grant to the extent possible.
f) A faculty member (as Principal or Co -Investigator) can undertake a maximum of two

application (Annexure 1) for approval of Research Grant to the extent possible.
f) A faculty member (as Principal or Co -Investigator) can undertake a maximum of two
research gran ts from the Institute at any point in time provided that out of two grants at
least one is an SGRP. An MGRP or LGRP has two stages of completion viz. provisional
completion and permanent completion ( Section 4 ). An investigator can apply for one
more MGRP o r LGRP after provisionally completing the existing MGRP or LGRP.
However an investigator can have at the most two provisionally completed MGRPs or
LGRPs at any time and can apply for further MGRP or LGRP only after one of the two
provisionally completed gr ants is permanently completed.
g) It is the responsibility of the Principal Investigator to be cognizant of the ethical issues in
the conduct and dissemination of research.
h) It will be the responsibility of the Principal Investigator to manage the financial m atters

the conduct and dissemination of research.
h) It will be the responsibility of the Principal Investigator to manage the financial m atters
related to the project. Up -to-date information about financial matters is available with the
Office of the Dean and hence the Project Investigator may directly access this
information from the Office of Dean. However all requests for advance or reim bursement
should be routed through Research Chair’s office with a copy of the grant approval
attached.

9. Process, Approval & Review
g) The proposal for an LGRP (Annexure 1 to be submitted in soft and hard copies )
will first be submitted to Research Committee (RC) for its approval. The Research
Committee may seek clarifications from the Principal Investigator before deciding
on the nominations to the Expert Evaluation Committee (EEC ) to review and report
on the pro posal.

h) In the case when all members of the project team are from within a particular Area,
the EEC will be constituted of two members from the applicants’ Area/related Area
and two members from other Areas.

i) In the case of a cross Area project team, the E EC will be constituted of one member
from each distinct Area of the corresponding team members and two members from
outside the represented Areas.

j) A copy of the evaluation report is sent to the Principal Investigator for modifying
and revising the applica tion duly addressing the evaluators’ concerns.

j) A copy of the evaluation report is sent to the Principal Investigator for modifying
and revising the applica tion duly addressing the evaluators’ concerns.

k) The revised application is recommended by the RCP Committee for the approval of
Dean and President.

l) The Principal Investigator and other investigators are to conduct a project
presentation before the EEC o n intimation from the RC Office every year after the
approval of the project.
10. Completion of LGRP

a) Completion stages: The completion of LGRP involves two stages: 1. Provisional
completion (Project Presentation and meeting the output requirements of
provisional completion). 2. Final completion (Meeting the output requirements of
LGRP).

b) Project Presentation: A presentatio n by the Principal Investigator is required to be
conducted before a larger body, including the EEC, the SOM -BMU faculty and the
research fellows.

b) Project Presentation: A presentatio n by the Principal Investigator is required to be
conducted before a larger body, including the EEC, the SOM -BMU faculty and the
research fellows.

c) Project Report Submission: Subsequent to the presentation, a detailed project
report incorporating the comme nts by EEC should be submitted in the specified
format for provisional completion ( Annexure 3 ). Achievement of output
requirement should be communicated in the specified format for permanent
completion ( Annexure 4 ).

d) Output requirement: Output requirement for permanent completion of an LGRP
is an International Conference Presentation/ Publication and a Research Article /


Case published in a peer-reviewed International Journal/ Case Clearing House.
Output requirement for provisional completion is an BMU Wor king Paper and
proof of submission to a peer -reviewed International Journal/ Case Clearing House.

11. Extension of LGRP
e) The maximum period of extension that can be granted to any existing LGRP will
be for one year. Approval of the extension will be sought using the format
attached in Annexure 2 .

f) If an existing LGRP is not completed within the approved period, including
extension, then the Principal Investigator will not be eligible to undertake any
other new institutional research grant projects.
12. Project Identification / Acknowledgement
Each LGRP would be provided a unique grant number by the Dean’s office which must be
quoted in all future communication. In the event of any publications emerging out of the
LGRP the authors should acknowledge the grant using the grant number provided.
7. Leaving BMU
The Principal Investigator is required to provisionally complete an LGRP before
leaving BMU. If s/he is leaving BMU prior to provisional completion of the project, either an
internal member of the research team would assume the role of the Principal Investigator and
continue the project or the project needs to be surrende red to the Research Chair’s office, in
writing and stating the reasons for surrender. The Principal Investigator of the surrendered

continue the project or the project needs to be surrende red to the Research Chair’s office, in
writing and stating the reasons for surrender. The Principal Investigator of the surrendered
project is required to return all funds drawn from the research grant before his/her departure
from the school.
